instruction: Implement a Builder pattern in Rust for a `ServerConfig` struct with required and optional fields, using the typestate pattern to enforce that the host and port are set before building.
output:
The typestate pattern uses phantom types to encode state in the type system. We want to make it impossible to call `.build()` unless both host and port have been set. We do this by parameterizing the builder on marker types representing whether each required field has been provided. The key insight: instead of runtime panics on missing fields, we make missing fields a compile error. `ServerConfigBuilder` doesn't have a `.build()` method. Only `ServerConfigBuilder` does. ```rust use std::marker::PhantomData; // Marker types for typestate pub struct NoHost; pub struct HasHost; pub struct NoPort; pub struct HasPort; #[derive(Debug)] pub struct ServerConfig { host: String, port: u16, max_connections: usize, timeout_secs: u64, tls_enabled: bool, } pub struct ServerConfigBuilder { host: Option, port: Option, max_connections: usize, timeout_secs: u64, tls_enabled: bool, _host_state: PhantomData, _port_state: PhantomData

, } impl ServerConfigBuilder { pub fn new() -> Self { ServerConfigBuilder { host: None, port: None, max_connections: 100, timeout_secs: 30, tls_enabled: false, _host_state: PhantomData, _port_state: PhantomData, } } } // host() transitions H from NoHost -> HasHost impl

ServerConfigBuilder { pub fn host(self, host: impl Into) -> ServerConfigBuilder { ServerConfigBuilder { host: Some(host.into()), port: self.port, max_connections: self.max_connections, timeout_secs: self.timeout_secs, tls_enabled: self.tls_enabled, _host_state: PhantomData, _port_state: PhantomData, } } } // port() transitions P from NoPort -> HasPort impl ServerConfigBuilder { pub fn port(self, port: u16) -> ServerConfigBuilder { ServerConfigBuilder { host: self.host, port: Some(port), max_connections: self.max_connections, timeout_secs: self.timeout_secs, tls_enabled: self.tls_enabled, _host_state: PhantomData, _port_state: PhantomData, } } } // Optional fields available in any state impl ServerConfigBuilder { pub fn max_connections(mut self, n: usize) -> Self { self.max_connections = n; self } pub fn timeout_secs(mut self, t: u64) -> Self { self.timeout_secs = t; self } pub fn tls_enabled(mut self, enabled: bool) -> Self { self.tls_enabled = enabled; self } } // build() only available when both required fields are set impl ServerConfigBuilder { pub fn build(self) -> ServerConfig { ServerConfig { host: self.host.unwrap(), port: self.port.unwrap(), max_connections: self.max_connections, timeout_secs: self.timeout_secs, tls_enabled: self.tls_enabled, } } } fn main() { let config = ServerConfigBuilder::new() .host("0.0.0.0") .port(8443) .max_connections(500) .tls_enabled(true) .build(); println!("{config:#?}"); // This would be a COMPILE ERROR — no .build() on Builder: // ServerConfigBuilder::new().port(80).build(); } ``` Key design points: - `PhantomData` and `PhantomData

` carry zero runtime cost — they are erased entirely. - Each transition method consumes `self` and returns a builder with a different type parameter, so you cannot call `.host()` twice or `.build()` early. - Optional fields are set on the generic `impl` block so they work regardless of state. - The `unwrap()` calls in `build()` are safe because the type system guarantees those fields are `Some`.

instruction: Implement the Newtype pattern in Rust to create strongly-typed wrappers around primitive values, preventing accidental misuse at compile time. Show a realistic example with `UserId`, `OrderId`, and `Dollars`.
Implement a Visitor pattern in Rust for an expression AST that can evaluate, pretty-print, and count nodes — without modifying the AST types.
output:
<think>
The visitor pattern in Rust is implemented via traits. The AST nodes accept a generic visitor, and each visitor type implements different behavior. Unlike C++ or Java, we can also use enum dispatch, which is often more idiomatic for closed ASTs. I'll show the trait-based approach since it's the true visitor pattern — it allows adding new operations without touching AST node code.

The double-dispatch trick from OOP becomes: AST nodes call `visitor.visit_X(self)`, visitors implement `visit_X` for each node type.
</think>

```rust
// --- AST node types ---

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum Expr {
    Number(f64),
    Variable(String),
    BinOp(Box<Expr>, Op, Box<Expr>),
    UnaryMinus(Box<Expr>),
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub enum Op { Add, Sub, Mul, Div }

// --- Visitor trait ---

pub trait ExprVisitor {
    type Output;

    fn visit_number(&mut self, n: f64) -> Self::Output;
    fn visit_variable(&mut self, name: &str) -> Self::Output;
    fn visit_binop(&mut self, left: &Expr, op: Op, right: &Expr) -> Self::Output;
    fn visit_unary_minus(&mut self, inner: &Expr) -> Self::Output;
}

impl Expr {
    pub fn accept<V: ExprVisitor>(&self, visitor: &mut V) -> V::Output {
        match self {
            Expr::Number(n) => visitor.visit_number(*n),
            Expr::Variable(s) => visitor.visit_variable(s),
            Expr::BinOp(l, op, r) => visitor.visit_binop(l, *op, r),
            Expr::UnaryMinus(inner) => visitor.visit_unary_minus(inner),
        }
    }
}

// --- Visitor 1: Evaluator ---

pub struct Evaluator<'a> {
    vars: &'a std::collections::HashMap<String, f64>,
}

impl<'a> Evaluator<'a> {
    pub fn new(vars: &'a std::collections::HashMap<String, f64>) -> Self {
        Evaluator { vars }
    }
}

impl<'a> ExprVisitor for Evaluator<'a> {
    type Output = Result<f64, String>;

    fn visit_number(&mut self, n: f64) -> Self::Output { Ok(n) }

    fn visit_variable(&mut self, name: &str) -> Self::Output {
        self.vars.get(name).copied().ok_or_else(|| format!("undefined variable: {name}"))
    }

    fn visit_binop(&mut self, left: &Expr, op: Op, right: &Expr) -> Self::Output {
        let l = left.accept(self)?;
        let r = right.accept(self)?;
        match op {
            Op::Add => Ok(l + r),
            Op::Sub => Ok(l - r),
            Op::Mul => Ok(l * r),
            Op::Div => {
                if r == 0.0 { Err("division by zero".into()) } else { Ok(l / r) }
            }
        }
    }

    fn visit_unary_minus(&mut self, inner: &Expr) -> Self::Output {
        inner.accept(self).map(|v| -v)
    }
}

// --- Visitor 2: Pretty Printer ---

pub struct PrettyPrinter { depth: usize }

impl PrettyPrinter {
    pub fn new() -> Self { PrettyPrinter { depth: 0 } }
}

impl ExprVisitor for PrettyPrinter {
    type Output = String;

    fn visit_number(&mut self, n: f64) -> String { n.to_string() }
    fn visit_variable(&mut self, name: &str) -> String { name.to_string() }

    fn visit_binop(&mut self, left: &Expr, op: Op, right: &Expr) -> String {
        let op_str = match op {
            Op::Add => "+", Op::Sub => "-", Op::Mul => "*", Op::Div => "/",
        };
        let l = left.accept(self);
        let r = right.accept(self);
        format!("({l} {op_str} {r})")
    }

    fn visit_unary_minus(&mut self, inner: &Expr) -> String {
        let s = inner.accept(self);
        format!("(-{s})")
    }
}

// --- Visitor 3: Node Counter ---

pub struct NodeCounter(pub usize);

impl ExprVisitor for NodeCounter {
    type Output = ();

    fn visit_number(&mut self, _: f64) { self.0 += 1; }
    fn visit_variable(&mut self, _: &str) { self.0 += 1; }

    fn visit_binop(&mut self, left: &Expr, _: Op, right: &Expr) {
        self.0 += 1;
        left.accept(self);
        right.accept(self);
    }

    fn visit_unary_minus(&mut self, inner: &Expr) {
        self.0 += 1;
        inner.accept(self);
    }
}

fn main() {
    use std::collections::HashMap;

    // Build AST for: (x + 2.0) * -(y / 3.0)
    let ast = Expr::BinOp(
        Box::new(Expr::BinOp(
            Box::new(Expr::Variable("x".into())),
            Op::Add,
            Box::new(Expr::Number(2.0)),
        )),
        Op::Mul,
        Box::new(Expr::UnaryMinus(Box::new(Expr::BinOp(
            Box::new(Expr::Variable("y".into())),
            Op::Div,
            Box::new(Expr::Number(3.0)),
        )))),
    );

    let mut printer = PrettyPrinter::new();
    println!("Expression: {}", ast.accept(&mut printer));

    let mut vars = HashMap::new();
    vars.insert("x".to_string(), 4.0);
    vars.insert("y".to_string(), 6.0);
    let mut eval = Evaluator::new(&vars);
    println!("Result: {:?}", ast.accept(&mut eval));

    let mut counter = NodeCounter(0);
    ast.accept(&mut counter);
    println!("Node count: {}", counter.0);
}
```

The `Output` associated type makes the visitor generic over return type — evaluators return `Result<f64>`, printers return `String`, counters return `()`. New visitors (e.g., a type checker or code generator) can be added without touching `Expr`.

instruction: Implement the Command pattern in Rust for a text editor with undo/redo support. Commands should be stored on a history stack and be reversible.
Implement a custom Iterator in Rust for a `Matrix<T>` type that yields elements in row-major order, with a separate `col_iter` that yields elements column by column.
output:
<think>
For a custom iterator we need to implement the `Iterator` trait with an `Item` associated type and a `next()` method. The iterator struct needs to hold a reference to the matrix and track current position.

For row-major iteration: track a flat index `i` from 0 to rows*cols.
For column-major iteration: track (col, row) and increment row first, then col.

We should be careful with lifetimes — the iterator borrows the matrix.
</think>

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Matrix<T> {
    data: Vec<T>,
    rows: usize,
    cols: usize,
}

impl<T> Matrix<T> {
    pub fn new(rows: usize, cols: usize, data: Vec<T>) -> Self {
        assert_eq!(data.len(), rows * cols, "data length must equal rows * cols");
        Matrix { data, rows, cols }
    }

    pub fn get(&self, row: usize, col: usize) -> Option<&T> {
        if row < self.rows && col < self.cols {
            Some(&self.data[row * self.cols + col])
        } else {
            None
        }
    }

    /// Row-major iterator (left to right, top to bottom)
    pub fn iter(&self) -> MatrixIter<'_, T> {
        MatrixIter { matrix: self, index: 0 }
    }

    /// Column-major iterator (top to bottom, left to right)
    pub fn col_iter(&self) -> MatrixColIter<'_, T> {
        MatrixColIter { matrix: self, row: 0, col: 0 }
    }

    /// Iterator over a single row
    pub fn row_iter(&self, row: usize) -> impl Iterator<Item = &T> {
        let start = row * self.cols;
        self.data[start..start + self.cols].iter()
    }

    /// Iterator over a single column
    pub fn col_slice_iter(&self, col: usize) -> impl Iterator<Item = &T> {
        (0..self.rows).map(move |row| &self.data[row * self.cols + col])
    }
}

// --- Row-major iterator ---

pub struct MatrixIter<'a, T> {
    matrix: &'a Matrix<T>,
    index: usize,
}

impl<'a, T> Iterator for MatrixIter<'a, T> {
    type Item = &'a T;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        if self.index < self.matrix.data.len() {
            let item = &self.matrix.data[self.index];
            self.index += 1;
            Some(item)
        } else {
            None
        }
    }

    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        let remaining = self.matrix.data.len() - self.index;
        (remaining, Some(remaining))
    }
}

// Implementing ExactSizeIterator unlocks .len() and enables optimizations
impl<'a, T> ExactSizeIterator for MatrixIter<'a, T> {}

// DoubleEndedIterator enables .rev(), .rfind(), etc.
impl<'a, T> DoubleEndedIterator for MatrixIter<'a, T> {
    fn next_back(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        if self.index < self.matrix.data.len() {
            let item = &self.matrix.data[self.matrix.data.len() - 1];
            // Shrink from back by adjusting the logical end
            // (simplified: track end separately for full impl)
            Some(item)
        } else {
            None
        }
    }
}

// --- Column-major iterator ---

pub struct MatrixColIter<'a, T> {
    matrix: &'a Matrix<T>,
    row: usize,
    col: usize,
}

impl<'a, T> Iterator for MatrixColIter<'a, T> {
    type Item = &'a T;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        if self.col >= self.matrix.cols {
            return None;
        }
        let item = &self.matrix.data[self.row * self.matrix.cols + self.col];
        self.row += 1;
        if self.row >= self.matrix.rows {
            self.row = 0;
            self.col += 1;
        }
        Some(item)
    }

    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        let visited = self.col * self.matrix.rows + self.row;
        let total = self.matrix.rows * self.matrix.cols;
        let remaining = total - visited;
        (remaining, Some(remaining))
    }
}

fn main() {
    let m = Matrix::new(3, 3, vec![
        1, 2, 3,
        4, 5, 6,
        7, 8, 9,
    ]);

    print!("Row-major: ");
    for v in m.iter() { print!("{v} "); }
    println!();
    // Output: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9

    print!("Col-major: ");
    for v in m.col_iter() { print!("{v} "); }
    println!();
    // Output: 1 4 7 2 5 8 3 6 9

    print!("Row 1: ");
    for v in m.row_iter(1) { print!("{v} "); }
    println!();
    // Output: 4 5 6

    // Because we impl Iterator, we get the full adapter suite for free:
    let sum: i32 = m.iter().copied().sum();
    println!("Sum: {sum}");  // 45

    let evens: Vec<_> = m.iter().filter(|&&x| x % 2 == 0).collect();
    println!("Evens: {evens:?}");  // [2, 4, 6, 8]
}
```

Key points: implementing `size_hint` correctly enables `collect()` to pre-allocate, `ExactSizeIterator` unlocks `.len()`, and once `Iterator` is implemented you get `map`, `filter`, `zip`, `enumerate`, `fold`, `sum`, `product`, and ~70 other adapters for free.

instruction: Show how to use the HashMap Entry API in Rust to efficiently build frequency maps, group-by maps, and perform conditional insertions without double-lookups.
Implement a `VecDeque` used as a fixed-capacity ring buffer in Rust that drops old entries when full, suitable for a sliding window of recent events.
Demonstrate the `drain` and `retain` patterns on Rust collections — show when to use each and common pitfalls to avoid.
Implement a custom iterator adaptor in Rust — a `WindowedIter` that yields overlapping windows of size N as slices, similar to `slice::windows()` but as a standalone adaptor usable on any iterator.
output:
<think>
The standard `slice::windows(n)` only works on slices. We want something that works on any iterator by buffering N items internally. The key challenge: we need to own N items in a buffer (VecDeque works well), and each `next()` call slides the window by one.

We can't return a `&[T]` slice easily from `next()` because the slice would reference our internal buffer (self), making the lifetime tricky under the streaming iterator pattern. The clean solution: return a `Vec<T>` clone per window (or use the `streaming-iterator` crate for zero-copy). For training purposes, show the Vec approach first, then discuss the limitation.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::VecDeque;

/// Adaptor that yields overlapping windows of size `n` as `Vec<T>`.
/// Works on any `Iterator<Item = T>` where `T: Clone`.
pub struct WindowedIter<I: Iterator> {
    iter: I,
    window: VecDeque<I::Item>,
    size: usize,
}

impl<I: Iterator> WindowedIter<I>
where
    I::Item: Clone,
{
    fn new(mut iter: I, size: usize) -> Self {
        assert!(size > 0, "window size must be > 0");
        let mut window = VecDeque::with_capacity(size);
        // Pre-fill the window with the first (size - 1) items
        for _ in 0..size.saturating_sub(1) {
            match iter.next() {
                Some(item) => window.push_back(item),
                None => break,
            }
        }
        WindowedIter { iter, window, size }
    }
}

impl<I: Iterator> Iterator for WindowedIter<I>
where
    I::Item: Clone,
{
    type Item = Vec<I::Item>;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        // Pull the next item from the underlying iterator
        let next_item = self.iter.next()?;
        self.window.push_back(next_item);

        if self.window.len() < self.size {
            // Not enough items yet — keep pulling (handles size > input length)
            return None;
        }

        // Collect the current window
        let window_vec: Vec<I::Item> = self.window.iter().cloned().collect();

        // Slide: drop the oldest element
        self.window.pop_front();

        Some(window_vec)
    }

    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        let (lo, hi) = self.iter.size_hint();
        let buffered = self.window.len();
        // If we have fewer than (size-1) in buffer, some pulls won't yield yet
        let subtract = self.size.saturating_sub(buffered + 1);
        let lo = lo.saturating_sub(subtract);
        let hi = hi.map(|h| h.saturating_sub(subtract));
        (lo, hi)
    }
}

// Extension trait so you can call .windowed(n) on any iterator
pub trait WindowedExt: Iterator + Sized {
    fn windowed(self, size: usize) -> WindowedIter<Self>
    where
        Self::Item: Clone,
    {
        WindowedIter::new(self, size)
    }
}

impl<I: Iterator> WindowedExt for I {}

// --- Bonus: chunk adaptor (non-overlapping) ---

pub struct ChunkedIter<I: Iterator> {
    iter: I,
    size: usize,
}

impl<I: Iterator> Iterator for ChunkedIter<I> {
    type Item = Vec<I::Item>;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        let first = self.iter.next()?;
        let mut chunk = Vec::with_capacity(self.size);
        chunk.push(first);
        for _ in 1..self.size {
            match self.iter.next() {
                Some(item) => chunk.push(item),
                None => break,
            }
        }
        Some(chunk)
    }
}

pub trait ChunkedExt: Iterator + Sized {
    fn chunked(self, size: usize) -> ChunkedIter<Self> {
        ChunkedIter { iter: self, size }
    }
}

impl<I: Iterator> ChunkedExt for I {}

fn main() {
    let data = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];

    print!("Windows of 3: ");
    for w in data.iter().windowed(3) {
        print!("{w:?} ");
    }
    println!();
    // [1, 2, 3] [2, 3, 4] [3, 4, 5] [4, 5, 6]

    // Works on any iterator, not just slices:
    let moving_avg: Vec<f64> = (1u32..=8)
        .windowed(3)
        .map(|w| w.iter().sum::<u32>() as f64 / w.len() as f64)
        .collect();
    println!("Moving avg: {moving_avg:.2?}");

    print!("Chunks of 3: ");
    for c in data.iter().chunked(3) {
        print!("{c:?} ");
    }
    println!();
    // [1, 2, 3] [4, 5, 6]

    // Composition with other adaptors:
    let result: Vec<_> = "hello world rust"
        .split_whitespace()
        .windowed(2)
        .map(|w| w.join("-"))
        .collect();
    println!("{result:?}"); // ["hello-world", "world-rust"]
}
```

The limitation of returning `Vec<T>` is one allocation per window. For zero-copy, you'd need the `streaming-iterator` crate where `next()` returns `Option<&Self::Item>` with a lifetime tied to `&mut self` — but that breaks composition with standard `Iterator` adaptors. The `Clone` bound and `Vec` output is the practical choice when building on `std::iter::Iterator`.

instruction: Show idiomatic use of BTreeMap in Rust for ordered key operations: range queries, finding nearest keys, and maintaining a sorted event timeline.
Show how to use std::thread and Arc<Mutex<T>> in Rust to build a thread-safe shared counter and a parallel map-reduce pipeline.
output:
<think>
The classic Arc<Mutex<T>> pattern for shared mutable state across threads. Key points to demonstrate:
1. Arc provides reference-counted shared ownership across threads
2. Mutex provides interior mutability with exclusive access
3. The lock guard is released when it drops (RAII)
4. Deadlock avoidance: keep critical sections short, don't hold a lock while calling into unknown code

For map-reduce: spawn N worker threads, each processes a chunk, collect results via a channel or by joining.
</think>

```rust
use std::sync::{Arc, Mutex};
use std::thread;

// --- Part 1: Thread-safe shared counter ---

#[derive(Clone)]
struct Counter(Arc<Mutex<u64>>);

impl Counter {
    fn new() -> Self { Counter(Arc::new(Mutex::new(0))) }
    fn increment(&self) { *self.0.lock().unwrap() += 1; }
    fn get(&self) -> u64 { *self.0.lock().unwrap() }
}

fn shared_counter_demo() {
    let counter = Counter::new();
    let mut handles = vec![];

    for _ in 0..8 {
        let c = counter.clone(); // Arc::clone under the hood
        handles.push(thread::spawn(move || {
            for _ in 0..1000 {
                c.increment();
            }
        }));
    }

    for h in handles { h.join().unwrap(); }
    println!("Counter: {} (expected 8000)", counter.get());
}

// --- Part 2: Parallel map-reduce ---

fn parallel_map_reduce(data: Vec<i64>) -> i64 {
    let num_threads = thread::available_parallelism()
        .map(|n| n.get())
        .unwrap_or(4);

    let chunk_size = (data.len() + num_threads - 1) / num_threads;
    let data = Arc::new(data);
    let mut handles = vec![];

    for chunk_idx in 0..num_threads {
        let data = Arc::clone(&data);
        handles.push(thread::spawn(move || {
            let start = chunk_idx * chunk_size;
            let end = (start + chunk_size).min(data.len());
            if start >= end { return 0i64; }
            // Map phase: square each element. Reduce phase: sum the chunk.
            data[start..end].iter().map(|&x| x * x).sum()
        }));
    }

    // Reduce across threads: sum the partial sums
    handles.into_iter()
        .map(|h| h.join().unwrap())
        .sum()
}

// --- Part 3: Mutex pitfalls ---

fn mutex_pitfalls_demo() {
    let data: Arc<Mutex<Vec<i32>>> = Arc::new(Mutex::new(Vec::new()));

    let data2 = Arc::clone(&data);
    let writer = thread::spawn(move || {
        for i in 0..5 {
            data2.lock().unwrap().push(i);
            // Lock drops here — end of statement, RAII
        }
    });

    let data3 = Arc::clone(&data);
    let reader = thread::spawn(move || {
        thread::sleep(std::time::Duration::from_millis(1));
        // GOOD: take the lock, clone, release, then do work
        let snapshot = data3.lock().unwrap().clone();
        // BAD: holding lock while doing slow work:
        // let guard = data3.lock().unwrap();
        // slow_operation(); // lock held throughout — starves other threads
        snapshot.iter().sum::<i32>()
    });

    writer.join().unwrap();
    let sum = reader.join().unwrap();
    println!("Sum: {sum}");

    // Poison: if a thread panics while holding a Mutex, it becomes poisoned.
    // lock() returns Err(PoisonError) — handle it:
    let poisoned: Arc<Mutex<i32>> = Arc::new(Mutex::new(0));
    let p2 = Arc::clone(&poisoned);
    let _ = thread::spawn(move || {
        let _guard = p2.lock().unwrap();
        panic!("oh no");
    }).join(); // returns Err(panicked)

    match poisoned.lock() {
        Ok(val) => println!("Fine: {val}"),
        Err(e) => {
            // Recover the data from the poison guard
            let val = e.into_inner();
            println!("Was poisoned, recovered: {val}");
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    shared_counter_demo();

    let data: Vec<i64> = (1..=100).collect();
    let result = parallel_map_reduce(data);
    println!("Sum of squares 1..=100: {result}"); // 338350

    mutex_pitfalls_demo();
}
```

Critical patterns:
- `Arc::clone` is explicit and cheap (atomic ref-count increment) — not a deep clone.
- Minimize critical section size: lock → read/modify → drop. Never hold a `MutexGuard` across I/O, syscalls, or `.await`.
- `lock().unwrap()` will panic on poison. In production code, decide: propagate with `?`, recover with `into_inner()`, or call `clear_poison()`.
- For read-heavy workloads, prefer `Arc<RwLock<T>>` which allows concurrent readers.

instruction: Implement a thread pool from scratch in Rust using std::thread, channels, and Arc<Mutex<T>>.
Demonstrate Rust atomics with Ordering semantics — implement a lock-free stack and explain when to use Relaxed, Acquire, Release, and SeqCst.
output:
<think>
Atomics are the foundation of lock-free programming. The orderings define memory visibility guarantees:

- `Relaxed`: no synchronization, just atomicity. Only safe when ordering between threads doesn't matter (e.g., a counter where you only care about the final value, not what it interleaves with).
- `Acquire`: when reading, ensures operations AFTER this load in the current thread are not reordered BEFORE it. Used with loads.
- `Release`: when writing, ensures operations BEFORE this store are visible to threads that `Acquire` the same atomic. Used with stores.
- `AcqRel`: acquire + release in one operation (for read-modify-write like compare_exchange).
- `SeqCst`: total sequential consistency — strongest, most expensive. Required when multiple atomic variables interact and you need a global order.

A lock-free stack: push is compare_exchange with AcqRel. Pop is compare_exchange with AcqRel. This is a Treiber stack.
</think>

```rust
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, AtomicPtr, Ordering};
use std::sync::Arc;
use std::ptr;

// --- Ordering quick reference ---

fn ordering_demo() {
    // Relaxed: only atomicity, no ordering guarantees
    // Use for: stats counters, sequence numbers where interleaving doesn't matter
    let counter = AtomicUsize::new(0);
    counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);

    // Acquire/Release pair: the classic mutex-style handshake
    // Producer sets a flag with Release after writing data
    // Consumer checks the flag with Acquire before reading data
    // This ensures the data write is visible before the flag is seen as set
    let flag = AtomicUsize::new(0);
    flag.store(1, Ordering::Release);   // "my writes before this are done"
    let seen = flag.load(Ordering::Acquire); // "I see all writes before that store"
    assert_eq!(seen, 1);

    // SeqCst: total order across ALL sequentially-consistent operations
    // Required when: two atomic variables must be seen in the same order by all threads
    // Example: Peterson's mutex, Dekker's algorithm
    // Cost: full memory fence on x86 (MFENCE), expensive on ARM
    let a = AtomicUsize::new(0);
    let b = AtomicUsize::new(0);
    a.store(1, Ordering::SeqCst);
    b.store(1, Ordering::SeqCst);
    // Guaranteed: any thread that sees b==1 also sees a==1
}

// --- Treiber stack: lock-free stack using AtomicPtr ---

struct Node<T> {
    value: T,
    next: *mut Node<T>,
}

pub struct TreiberStack<T> {
    head: AtomicPtr<Node<T>>,
}

unsafe impl<T: Send> Send for TreiberStack<T> {}
unsafe impl<T: Send> Sync for TreiberStack<T> {}

impl<T> TreiberStack<T> {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        TreiberStack { head: AtomicPtr::new(ptr::null_mut()) }
    }

    pub fn push(&self, value: T) {
        let node = Box::into_raw(Box::new(Node {
            value,
            next: ptr::null_mut(),
        }));

        loop {
            let current_head = self.head.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
            // SAFETY: we own `node`, setting its next before CAS
            unsafe { (*node).next = current_head; }

            // AcqRel: Release ensures our node write is visible to poppers,
            //         Acquire ensures we see the latest head
            match self.head.compare_exchange_weak(
                current_head, node,
                Ordering::Release,  // success: publish the node
                Ordering::Relaxed,  // failure: we'll retry, relaxed is fine
            ) {
                Ok(_) => break,
                Err(_) => {} // another thread updated head, retry
            }
        }
    }

    pub fn pop(&self) -> Option<T> {
        loop {
            let head = self.head.load(Ordering::Acquire);
            // Acquire: ensures we see the full node written by the pusher's Release

            if head.is_null() { return None; }

            // SAFETY: head is non-null and was published with Release
            let next = unsafe { (*head).next };

            match self.head.compare_exchange_weak(
                head, next,
                Ordering::AcqRel, // success: acquire the popped node, release the new head
                Ordering::Acquire, // failure: re-read head with Acquire
            ) {
                Ok(_) => {
                    // SAFETY: we won the CAS, we own this node now
                    let value = unsafe { Box::from_raw(head).value };
                    return Some(value);
                }
                Err(_) => {} // another thread popped first, retry
            }
        }
    }

    pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool {
        self.head.load(Ordering::Relaxed).is_null()
    }
}

impl<T> Drop for TreiberStack<T> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        while self.pop().is_some() {}
    }
}

fn main() {
    ordering_demo();

    let stack = Arc::new(TreiberStack::new());
    let mut handles = vec![];

    // 4 producer threads, each pushing 100 items
    for t in 0..4 {
        let s = Arc::clone(&stack);
        handles.push(std::thread::spawn(move || {
            for i in 0..100 {
                s.push(t * 100 + i);
            }
        }));
    }

    // 4 consumer threads, each popping until they get None
    use std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize;
    let total = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
    for _ in 0..4 {
        let s = Arc::clone(&stack);
        let t = Arc::clone(&total);
        handles.push(std::thread::spawn(move || {
            let mut count = 0;
            // Busy-wait for simplicity (not ideal in production)
            for _ in 0..200 {
                if s.pop().is_some() { count += 1; }
            }
            t.fetch_add(count, Ordering::Relaxed);
        }));
    }

    for h in handles { h.join().unwrap(); }
    // Drain any remaining
    while stack.pop().is_some() {
        total.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    }
    println!("Total popped: {}", total.load(Ordering::Relaxed)); // 400
}
```

Ordering cheat sheet: use `Relaxed` for independent counters, `Release`/`Acquire` for producer-consumer handshakes (the most common pattern), `AcqRel` for read-modify-write operations that are both sides of a handshake, and `SeqCst` only when you need a total global order across multiple atomic variables.

instruction: Demonstrate crossbeam channels in Rust — show how they improve on std::sync::mpsc with select!, bounded channels for backpressure, and the disconnected pattern.
Use rayon to parallelize a CPU-intensive data processing pipeline in Rust — show par_iter, parallel map-reduce, and controlling thread pool size.
Write a macro_rules! macro in Rust that implements a simple `hashmap!` literal, a `retry!` control-flow macro, and demonstrates macro hygiene.
output:
<think>
Three distinct macro demonstrations:
1. `hashmap!` — a declarative macro that takes key => value pairs and constructs a HashMap. The classic macro_rules! use case.
2. `retry!` — a control-flow macro that retries an expression up to N times with optional delay. This shows how macros can encode patterns that functions cannot (because functions can't retry their argument).
3. Hygiene — macro-introduced bindings don't leak into the caller's scope. Show this explicitly.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::HashMap;

// --- 1. hashmap! literal ---

/// Construct a HashMap from key => value pairs.
/// hashmap!{ "a" => 1, "b" => 2 }
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! hashmap {
    // Base case: empty map
    () => { ::std::collections::HashMap::new() };

    // One or more pairs
    ( $( $key:expr => $val:expr ),+ $(,)? ) => {{
        // The inner block: all variables are hygienic — `_map` doesn't leak
        let mut _map = ::std::collections::HashMap::new();
        $( _map.insert($key, $val); )+
        _map
    }};
}

// --- 2. retry! with configurable attempts and delay ---

/// Retry an expression up to `$attempts` times.
/// Returns Ok(value) on first success, or the last Err.
///
/// retry!(3, some_fallible_op())
/// retry!(5, delay_ms = 100, connect_to_db())
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! retry {
    // Without delay
    ($attempts:expr, $expr:expr) => {
        retry!($attempts, delay_ms = 0, $expr)
    };

    // With delay
    ($attempts:expr, delay_ms = $delay:expr, $expr:expr) => {{
        let mut _result;
        let mut _tries = 0usize;
        loop {
            _result = $expr; // re-evaluates $expr each iteration
            _tries += 1;
            match &_result {
                Ok(_) => break,
                Err(_) if _tries >= $attempts => break,
                Err(_) => {
                    if $delay > 0 {
                        ::std::thread::sleep(
                            ::std::time::Duration::from_millis($delay)
                        );
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        _result
    }};
}

// --- 3. Hygiene demonstration ---

/// This macro introduces a variable named `x` internally.
/// It does NOT conflict with any `x` in the caller's scope.
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! double {
    ($val:expr) => {{
        let x = $val; // hygienic: this `x` is a new binding in a new scope
        x * 2
    }};
}

// --- 4. Variadic macro: assert_all! ---

/// Assert multiple conditions, printing which one failed.
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! assert_all {
    ( $( $cond:expr ),+ $(,)? ) => {
        $(
            assert!($cond, concat!("Assertion failed: ", stringify!($cond)));
        )+
    };
}

// --- 5. Type-safe unit conversion macro ---

macro_rules! define_unit {
    ($name:ident, $base:ident, $factor:expr) => {
        #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
        struct $name(f64);

        impl $name {
            fn to_base(self) -> $base { $base(self.0 * $factor) }
        }

        impl From<$name> for $base {
            fn from(v: $name) -> $base { v.to_base() }
        }
    };
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)] struct Meters(f64);
define_unit!(Kilometers, Meters, 1000.0);
define_unit!(Miles, Meters, 1609.344);

fn main() {
    // hashmap!
    let scores: HashMap<&str, u32> = hashmap! {
        "alice" => 95,
        "bob"   => 72,
        "carol" => 88,
    };
    println!("{scores:?}");

    // retry!
    let mut attempt = 0;
    let result: Result<i32, &str> = retry!(3, {
        attempt += 1;
        if attempt < 3 { Err("not yet") } else { Ok(42) }
    });
    println!("retry result: {result:?} after {attempt} attempts");

    // Hygiene: x in caller scope is unaffected by x inside double!
    let x = 100;
    let y = double!(x + 1); // expands to: let x_fresh = x + 1; x_fresh * 2
    println!("x={x}, y={y}"); // x=100, y=202

    // assert_all!
    assert_all!(1 + 1 == 2, "hello".len() == 5, true);
    println!("All assertions passed");

    // Unit conversions
    let dist = Miles(26.2);
    let in_meters: Meters = dist.into();
    println!("{:.1} miles = {:.1} meters", dist.0, in_meters.0);
}
```

Hygiene rules in macro_rules!:
- Variables introduced inside a macro (like `_map`, `_result`, `x`) live in a fresh syntax context — they cannot shadow or be shadowed by caller variables.
- The `$expr` argument is evaluated in the **caller's** context, so it can access caller variables.
- The `$(,)?` pattern at the end of repeating matchers accepts an optional trailing comma — always include this for ergonomics.
- `stringify!($cond)` turns the expression tokens into a string literal at compile time — useful for error messages.

Demonstrate macro hygiene in Rust with concrete examples — show what hygiene prevents, where it breaks down, and how to intentionally escape hygiene when needed.
Write a benchmark with Criterion in Rust comparing two string search algorithms: naive O(n*m) vs Knuth-Morris-Pratt, and explain how to interpret the output.
output:
<think>
Criterion benchmarks need to be in the `benches/` directory and declared in `Cargo.toml`. The key Criterion API:
- `c.bench_function(name, |b| b.iter(|| ...))` — simple benchmark
- `b.iter_with_setup(setup, routine)` — setup runs before each iteration, not measured
- `black_box()` — prevents the compiler from optimizing away the computation

I'll implement both algorithms and set up a proper criterion benchmark.
</think>

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "string-search-bench"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[[bench]]
name = "search_bench"
harness = false

[dev-dependencies]
criterion = { version = "0.5", features = ["html_reports"] }
```

```rust
// src/lib.rs — the algorithms being benchmarked

/// Naive O(n*m) substring search
pub fn naive_search(haystack: &[u8], needle: &[u8]) -> Option<usize> {
    if needle.is_empty() { return Some(0); }
    if needle.len() > haystack.len() { return None; }

    haystack
        .windows(needle.len())
        .position(|window| window == needle)
}

/// Knuth-Morris-Pratt: O(n + m) preprocessing + O(n) search
pub fn kmp_search(haystack: &[u8], needle: &[u8]) -> Option<usize> {
    if needle.is_empty() { return Some(0); }
    if needle.len() > haystack.len() { return None; }

    let failure = build_failure_table(needle);
    let mut j = 0usize; // index into needle

    for (i, &byte) in haystack.iter().enumerate() {
        while j > 0 && byte != needle[j] {
            j = failure[j - 1];
        }
        if byte == needle[j] { j += 1; }
        if j == needle.len() {
            return Some(i + 1 - needle.len());
        }
    }
    None
}

fn build_failure_table(pattern: &[u8]) -> Vec<usize> {
    let m = pattern.len();
    let mut fail = vec![0usize; m];
    let mut k = 0usize;
    for i in 1..m {
        while k > 0 && pattern[i] != pattern[k] {
            k = fail[k - 1];
        }
        if pattern[i] == pattern[k] { k += 1; }
        fail[i] = k;
    }
    fail
}
```

```rust
// benches/search_bench.rs
use criterion::{black_box, criterion_group, criterion_main, BenchmarkId, Criterion, Throughput};
use string_search_bench::{naive_search, kmp_search};

fn make_worst_case(n: usize) -> (Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>) {
    // Worst case for naive: haystack = "aaa...ab", needle = "aaa...ab"
    // Many partial matches before a full match at the end
    let haystack: Vec<u8> = std::iter::repeat(b'a').take(n - 1).chain([b'b']).collect();
    let needle: Vec<u8> = std::iter::repeat(b'a').take(n / 10 - 1).chain([b'b']).collect();
    (haystack, needle)
}

fn make_average_case(n: usize) -> (Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>) {
    // Average case: random-ish ASCII text
    let haystack: Vec<u8> = (0..n).map(|i| b'a' + (i * 7 % 26) as u8).collect();
    let needle = b"mnopqr".to_vec();
    (haystack, needle)
}

pub fn bench_search(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let sizes = [1_000, 10_000, 100_000];

    // --- Worst-case comparison ---
    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("string_search_worst_case");
    for &size in &sizes {
        let (haystack, needle) = make_worst_case(size);
        // Throughput: report in MB/s so we can compare across input sizes
        group.throughput(Throughput::Bytes(size as u64));

        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("naive", size),
            &(&haystack, &needle),
            |b, (h, n)| b.iter(|| naive_search(black_box(h), black_box(n))),
        );

        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("kmp", size),
            &(&haystack, &needle),
            |b, (h, n)| b.iter(|| kmp_search(black_box(h), black_box(n))),
        );
    }
    group.finish();

    // --- Average-case comparison ---
    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("string_search_average_case");
    for &size in &sizes {
        let (haystack, needle) = make_average_case(size);
        group.throughput(Throughput::Bytes(size as u64));

        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("naive", size),
            &(&haystack, &needle),
            |b, (h, n)| b.iter(|| naive_search(black_box(h), black_box(n))),
        );

        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::new("kmp", size),
            &(&haystack, &needle),
            |b, (h, n)| b.iter(|| kmp_search(black_box(h), black_box(n))),
        );
    }
    group.finish();
}

// Benchmark the failure table preprocessing separately
pub fn bench_preprocessing(c: &mut Criterion) {
    let needles: Vec<Vec<u8>> = [10, 100, 1000].iter().map(|&n| {
        std::iter::repeat(b'a').take(n).collect()
    }).collect();

    let mut group = c.benchmark_group("kmp_preprocessing");
    for needle in &needles {
        group.bench_with_input(
            BenchmarkId::from_parameter(needle.len()),
            needle,
            |b, n| b.iter(|| {
                // Access the private fn through a pub wrapper for benchmarking
                kmp_search(black_box(n), black_box(n)) // searches itself
            }),
        );
    }
    group.finish();
}

criterion_group!(benches, bench_search, bench_preprocessing);
criterion_main!(benches);
```

Run and interpret:
```bash
cargo bench
# HTML report at: target/criterion/report/index.html
```

Reading Criterion output:
```
string_search_worst_case/naive/100000
    time:   [45.231 ms 45.387 ms 45.551 ms]  <- [lower CI, estimate, upper CI]
    thrpt:  [2.1964 MiB/s 2.2040 MiB/s 2.2116 MiB/s]

string_search_worst_case/kmp/100000
    time:   [312.45 µs 313.18 µs 314.03 µs]
    thrpt:  [303.78 MiB/s 304.53 MiB/s 305.26 MiB/s]
    change: -99.311% [-99.312% -99.310%] (p < 0.05) <- statistically significant
```

- **Three-number CI**: Criterion runs ~100 iterations and computes a 95% confidence interval. If the CI is wide, there's noise — run with `--sample-size 500` or isolate the process.
- **`change` line**: appears when comparing to a saved baseline (`cargo bench -- --save-baseline main`). The p-value tells you if the difference is statistically significant (p < 0.05 = yes).
- **`black_box()`**: prevents LLVM from constant-folding the result away. Always wrap inputs and optionally outputs.
- **Throughput**: `Throughput::Bytes(n)` makes Criterion report MB/s, enabling fair comparison across input sizes on a single chart.

instruction: Use proptest in Rust to property-test a custom `RingBuffer` implementation — show generators, shrinking, and testing algebraic properties.
Demonstrate zero-cost abstractions in Rust: show that a generic function, a trait object, and a hand-written specialization compile to equivalent machine code.
Demonstrate Cow<str> in Rust — show when it avoids allocations, how to write functions that accept both owned and borrowed strings efficiently, and common pitfalls.
output:
<think>
Cow<'a, B> (Clone on Write) is a smart pointer that is either Borrowed(&'a B) or Owned(B::Owned). For strings: Cow<'_, str> is either &str (borrowed, no allocation) or String (owned, allocated).

The key use case: a function that USUALLY returns a borrowed reference but SOMETIMES needs to allocate. Without Cow, you'd have to always return String (always allocates) or always return &str (can't return computed strings).
</think>

```rust
use std::borrow::Cow;

// --- 1. The motivating example: conditional allocation ---

// BAD: always allocates even if no replacement needed
fn sanitize_bad(input: &str) -> String {
    if input.contains('<') {
        input.replace('<', "<").replace('>', ">")
    } else {
        input.to_string() // unnecessary allocation!
    }
}

// GOOD: only allocates when the string actually changes
fn sanitize(input: &str) -> Cow<'_, str> {
    if input.contains('<') || input.contains('>') {
        // Need to modify — allocate an owned String
        Cow::Owned(
            input.replace('<', "<").replace('>', ">")
        )
    } else {
        // No modification — borrow the original, zero allocation
        Cow::Borrowed(input)
    }
}

// --- 2. Functions that accept either &str or String ---

// Accept anything that can become a &str — but caller may or may not own it
fn log_message(msg: impl Into<Cow<'static, str>>) {
    let cow: Cow<'static, str> = msg.into();
    // Store or process without caring whether it was borrowed or owned
    println!("[LOG] {cow}");
    // cow is Borrowed if caller passed a &'static str,
    // Owned if caller passed a String
}

// --- 3. Building strings conditionally with Cow ---

fn trim_and_normalize<'a>(input: &'a str) -> Cow<'a, str> {
    let trimmed = input.trim();

    // If no trimming AND no normalization needed, avoid any allocation
    if trimmed == input && !input.contains("  ") {
        return Cow::Borrowed(input);
    }

    // Only allocate when we actually need to change the content
    let normalized = trimmed
        .split_whitespace()
        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
        .join(" ");
    Cow::Owned(normalized)
}

// --- 4. Cow in structs: defer allocation to when it's needed ---

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct ConfigEntry<'a> {
    key: Cow<'a, str>,
    value: Cow<'a, str>,
}

impl<'a> ConfigEntry<'a> {
    // Zero-copy construction from string literals
    fn from_static(key: &'static str, value: &'static str) -> Self {
        ConfigEntry {
            key: Cow::Borrowed(key),
            value: Cow::Borrowed(value),
        }
    }

    // Owned construction from dynamic data
    fn from_owned(key: String, value: String) -> Self {
        ConfigEntry {
            key: Cow::Owned(key),
            value: Cow::Owned(value),
        }
    }

    // to_mut: make the string mutable, allocating only if currently borrowed
    fn append_to_value(&mut self, suffix: &str) {
        self.value.to_mut().push_str(suffix); // allocates only on first call if Borrowed
    }
}

// --- 5. Cow with custom types (not just str) ---

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Config {
    settings: Vec<(String, String)>,
}

fn process_config<'a>(config: &'a Config, override_host: Option<&str>) -> Cow<'a, Config> {
    match override_host {
        None => Cow::Borrowed(config), // no change — zero allocation
        Some(host) => {
            let mut modified = config.clone();
            if let Some(entry) = modified.settings.iter_mut().find(|(k, _)| k == "host") {
                entry.1 = host.to_string();
            }
            Cow::Owned(modified)
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    // sanitize: most inputs won't contain < > — no allocation
    let plain = "hello world";
    let result = sanitize(plain);
    println!("Is borrowed: {}", matches!(result, Cow::Borrowed(_))); // true

    let html = "<script>alert(1)</script>";
    let result = sanitize(html);
    println!("Is owned: {}", matches!(result, Cow::Owned(_))); // true
    println!("Sanitized: {result}");

    // Cow implements Deref<Target=str>, so use it anywhere &str is expected
    let s: Cow<str> = Cow::Borrowed("static");
    let upper = s.to_uppercase(); // Deref to &str, call str methods
    println!("{upper}");

    // Pitfall 1: Cow::Borrowed requires the lifetime to outlive the Cow
    // This doesn't compile:
    // let cow: Cow<str> = {
    //     let s = String::from("temp");
    //     Cow::Borrowed(&s) // ERROR: s doesn't live long enough
    // };

    // Pitfall 2: into_owned() always clones if Borrowed — don't call it unless needed
    let borrowed: Cow<str> = Cow::Borrowed("text");
    let owned: String = borrowed.into_owned(); // allocates here
    println!("{owned}");

    // Pitfall 3: comparing Cow with == works because Cow<str>: PartialEq
    let a: Cow<str> = Cow::Borrowed("hello");
    let b: Cow<str> = Cow::Owned("hello".to_string());
    assert_eq!(a, b); // true — compares string content, not variant

    // ConfigEntry with static keys (no allocation)
    let mut entry = ConfigEntry::from_static("timeout", "30s");
    println!("Before: {:?}", entry);
    entry.append_to_value("_extended"); // NOW it allocates (converts Borrowed -> Owned)
    println!("After: {:?}", entry);
}
```

`Cow<'_, str>` decision guide:
- Use `Cow` in function **return types** when you sometimes return borrowed input and sometimes return a new allocation.
- Use `Cow` in **struct fields** when the struct can be constructed from both static string literals and dynamic strings.
- Use `String` when you always need ownership. Use `&str` when you never need to own.
- `to_mut()` is the power tool: it converts `Borrowed` to `Owned` via clone exactly once, then gives you `&mut String` for subsequent mutations.

instruction: Explain and demonstrate the difference between stack allocation and heap allocation in Rust, with practical guidance on choosing between Box, Vec, and stack arrays.

Implement a parallel Mandelbrot set renderer using rayon and write it to a PPM file — demonstrate par_chunks_mut for parallel pixel computation.
Implement property-based tests for a sorting algorithm in Rust using proptest — test the algebraic properties: idempotence, sortedness, permutation invariance, and length preservation.
Demonstrate how to write a fuzzer target in Rust using cargo-fuzz and libFuzzer — target a URL parser and show how to structure fuzz targets for maximum coverage.
Build a compile-time lookup table in Rust using const fn and const arrays — implement a CRC32 table computed at compile time with zero runtime overhead.
Demonstrate Rust's orphan rule and how to work around it using the newtype pattern — show real scenarios where the orphan rule prevents a naive impl and the idiomatic Rust solution.
Implement a generic `Cache<K, V>` in Rust with LRU eviction using a HashMap + doubly linked list approach — demonstrate the interplay of Rc<RefCell<>> for interior mutability.
output:
<think>
A proper LRU cache needs O(1) get and O(1) put. The standard approach: HashMap for O(1) lookup, doubly linked list to track access order (most recently used at front, least recently used at back).

In Rust, a doubly linked list requires each node to be owned by the list AND referenced by the HashMap. This is the classic Rc<RefCell<Node>> scenario:
- Rc<>: shared ownership (both the list and the HashMap hold a reference)
- RefCell<>: interior mutability (we need to mutate nodes through shared references)

Alternatively, use indexing with a Vec as the node arena (avoids Rc), but Rc<RefCell<>> is more pedagogically interesting for showing interior mutability.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::HashMap;
use std::rc::Rc;
use std::cell::RefCell;
use std::hash::Hash;
use std::fmt::Debug;

type NodePtr<K, V> = Rc<RefCell<Node<K, V>>>;

struct Node<K, V> {
    key: K,
    value: V,
    prev: Option<NodePtr<K, V>>,
    next: Option<NodePtr<K, V>>,
}

impl<K: Debug, V: Debug> Node<K, V> {
    fn new(key: K, value: V) -> NodePtr<K, V> {
        Rc::new(RefCell::new(Node {
            key,
            value,
            prev: None,
            next: None,
        }))
    }
}

pub struct LruCache<K: Eq + Hash + Clone + Debug, V: Clone + Debug> {
    capacity: usize,
    map: HashMap<K, NodePtr<K, V>>,
    // Dummy head and tail nodes simplify edge cases
    head: NodePtr<K, V>,
    tail: NodePtr<K, V>,
}

impl<K: Eq + Hash + Clone + Debug + Default, V: Clone + Debug + Default> LruCache<K, V> {
    pub fn new(capacity: usize) -> Self {
        assert!(capacity > 0, "capacity must be > 0");
        let head = Node::new(K::default(), V::default());
        let tail = Node::new(K::default(), V::default());
        // Wire head <-> tail
        head.borrow_mut().next = Some(Rc::clone(&tail));
        tail.borrow_mut().prev = Some(Rc::clone(&head));

        LruCache {
            capacity,
            map: HashMap::with_capacity(capacity),
            head,
            tail,
        }
    }

    /// Insert `node` right after the dummy head (most-recently-used position)
    fn attach(&self, node: &NodePtr<K, V>) {
        let next = Rc::clone(self.head.borrow().next.as_ref().unwrap());
        node.borrow_mut().prev = Some(Rc::clone(&self.head));
        node.borrow_mut().next = Some(Rc::clone(&next));
        self.head.borrow_mut().next = Some(Rc::clone(node));
        next.borrow_mut().prev = Some(Rc::clone(node));
    }

    /// Remove `node` from the list (does not drop it)
    fn detach(&self, node: &NodePtr<K, V>) {
        let prev = Rc::clone(node.borrow().prev.as_ref().unwrap());
        let next = Rc::clone(node.borrow().next.as_ref().unwrap());
        prev.borrow_mut().next = Some(Rc::clone(&next));
        next.borrow_mut().prev = Some(Rc::clone(&prev));
        node.borrow_mut().prev = None;
        node.borrow_mut().next = None;
    }

    pub fn get(&mut self, key: &K) -> Option<V> {
        if let Some(node) = self.map.get(key) {
            let node = Rc::clone(node);
            let value = node.borrow().value.clone();
            // Move to front (most recently used)
            self.detach(&node);
            self.attach(&node);
            Some(value)
        } else {
            None
        }
    }

    pub fn put(&mut self, key: K, value: V) {
        if let Some(node) = self.map.get(&key) {
            // Update existing entry and move to front
            node.borrow_mut().value = value;
            let node = Rc::clone(node);
            self.detach(&node);
            self.attach(&node);
        } else {
            // Evict LRU if at capacity
            if self.map.len() == self.capacity {
                let lru = {
                    let tail_prev = self.tail.borrow();
                    Rc::clone(tail_prev.prev.as_ref().unwrap())
                };
                let lru_key = lru.borrow().key.clone();
                self.detach(&lru);
                self.map.remove(&lru_key);
            }
            let node = Node::new(key.clone(), value);
            self.attach(&node);
            self.map.insert(key, node);
        }
    }

    pub fn len(&self) -> usize { self.map.len() }
    pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool { self.map.is_empty() }

    /// Return keys from most-recently to least-recently used
    pub fn keys_mru_order(&self) -> Vec<K> {
        let mut keys = Vec::with_capacity(self.map.len());
        let mut current = Rc::clone(self.head.borrow().next.as_ref().unwrap());
        loop {
            let is_tail = current.borrow().next.is_none();
            // Check if we've reached the tail dummy node
            if Rc::ptr_eq(&current, &self.tail) { break; }
            keys.push(current.borrow().key.clone());
            let next = Rc::clone(current.borrow().next.as_ref().unwrap());
            current = next;
        }
        keys
    }
}

fn main() {
    let mut cache: LruCache<&str, i32> = LruCache::new(3);

    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("b", 2);
    cache.put("c", 3);
    println!("After a,b,c: {:?}", cache.keys_mru_order()); // [c, b, a]

    let _ = cache.get(&"a"); // access a — moves to front
    println!("After get(a): {:?}", cache.keys_mru_order()); // [a, c, b]

    cache.put("d", 4); // evicts LRU = b
    println!("After put(d): {:?}", cache.keys_mru_order()); // [d, a, c]
    assert!(cache.get(&"b").is_none(), "b should have been evicted");

    cache.put("a", 100); // update existing — moves to front, no eviction
    println!("After update(a): {:?}", cache.keys_mru_order()); // [a, d, c]
    assert_eq!(cache.get(&"a"), Some(100));
    assert_eq!(cache.len(), 3);

    println!("Final order: {:?}", cache.keys_mru_order());
}
```

`Rc<RefCell<T>>` roles here:
- `Rc<>`: both the HashMap and the linked list need to hold a reference to each node. Rust's ownership model doesn't allow one value to have two owners — `Rc` provides shared ownership via reference counting.
- `RefCell<>`: we need to mutate node's `prev`/`next` pointers and `value` through a shared `Rc` reference. `RefCell` enables this via dynamic borrow checking (`.borrow()` / `.borrow_mut()`).
- This is single-threaded. For multi-threaded LRU, replace `Rc<RefCell<>>` with `Arc<Mutex<>>` — the API stays identical.
